# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
List the **four histological layers** of the human oesophagus and state the primary cell type found in each.

**Model Answer:**

The four layers from lumen outward are:

1. **Mucosa** — non-keratinised **stratified squamous epithelial cells** providing a friction-resistant lining.
2. **Submucosa** — **fibroblasts** and connective tissue housing the **Meissner's (submucosal) nerve plexus**, blood vessels, and mucous glands.
3. **Muscularis Propria** — arranged as inner circular and outer longitudinal layers of **smooth muscle cells** (distal two-thirds) and **skeletal muscle fibres** (proximal one-third).
4. **Adventitia** — **fibroblasts** producing type I collagen anchoring the oesophagus to surrounding mediastinal structures. *(Notably, the oesophagus has **no serosa**, which contributes to its poor surgical suturing strength.)* **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
Define **decellularisation** and mention **two advantages** and **one limitation** of using decellularised oesophageal matrix (DHO) as a tissue-engineered scaffold.

**Model Answer:**

**Decellularisation** is the process of removing all cellular and nuclear material from a donor organ while preserving the native **extracellular matrix (ECM)** architecture, mechanical integrity, and bioactive cues (e.g., growth factors, glycosaminoglycans).

**Two advantages:**
- **Native 3D ECM geometry** is preserved, providing physiologically relevant cues for cell attachment and differentiation. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Reduced immunogenicity** since cellular antigens (MHC molecules) are removed; the residual ECM is well tolerated, especially after cross-species protocols. *(1 mark)*

**One limitation:**
- **Donor scarcity** and **batch-to-batch variability** in ECM composition hinder GMP-compliant scale-up. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students frequently **skip the diagram** while explaining scaffold fabrication. Always include a *labelled schematic* (1–2 marks reserved for diagrams in KTU 2024 scheme).
> 2. For 14-mark questions, students write vague statements like *"cells are added to scaffold"* — instead, state **cell type, seeding density, and seeding method** (static/dynamic/perfusion).
> 3. **Units** are mandatory in numerical derivations; missing units cost 0.5–1 mark per step.
> 4. Always **state assumptions** (laminar flow, steady state, incompressible fluid) at the start of any derivation.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **clinical need** for oesophageal tissue engineering with reference to at least **three pathological conditions** requiring oesophageal replacement. Compare the **conventional surgical approaches** with **tissue engineering alternatives**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step fabrication of a bilayered tissue-engineered oesophageal construct** using **electrospun PCL + collagen hydrogel**, specifying cell types, scaffold architecture, and the role of **dynamic bioreactor preconditioning**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Clinical Need & Conventional vs Tissue Engineering Comparison**

**Three pathological conditions:**
1. **Oesophageal Cancer** (SCC/Adenocarcinoma) — globally the **7th most common cancer**; oesophagectomy leaves a long gap. *(1 mark)*
2. **Corrosive/Caustic injury** — acid/alkali ingestion causes full-thickness necrosis, predominantly in paediatric populations of developing nations. *(1 mark)*
3. **Congenital Oesophageal Atresia** — failure of the foregut to canalise, with an incidence of **~1 in 4,000 live births**. *(1 mark)*

**Conventional surgical approaches:**
- **Gastric pull-up (oesophagogastrostomy):** Stomach is mobilised and pulled into the chest. Disadvantage — high anastomotic leak rate (~10–15%), reflux, pulmonary complications. *(1 mark)*
- **Colon interposition:** A segment of colon is used as a conduit. Disadvantage — redundant motility, mucus production, long-term redundancy risk. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Skin/visceral flaps (e.g., radial forearm, jejunum):** Multi-stage procedures with significant donor-site morbidity. *(0.5 mark)*

**Tissue engineering advantages over conventional methods:**
- Uses **autologous cells**, eliminating chronic immune rejection.
- **Biodegradable scaffolds** that remodel into native tissue — no permanent foreign body.
- **Tailored geometry** — patient-specific sizing via medical imaging.
- **Potential for off-the-shelf cryopreserved decellularised grafts** in emergencies. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Bilayered Construct Fabrication**

**Step 1 — Inner Mucosal Layer: Collagen Hydrogel**
- Prepare a **1.5% type I collagen solution** neutralised with NaOH and mixed with DMEM.
- Seed with **autologous oral mucosal keratinocytes** at a density of **1 × 10⁶ cells/mL**.
- Crosslink thermally at 37 °C for 30 min to form a gel.
- The hydrogel provides a soft, cell-adhesive substrate mimicking native mucosal ECM. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Outer Structural Layer: Electrospun PCL Mesh**
- Dissolve **PCL (Mw ~80 kDa)** in a 1:4 chloroform/methanol mixture at 12% w/v.
- Electrospin at **15 kV, 1.5 mL/h flow, 15 cm tip-to-collector distance** onto a rotating mandrel (5 mm diameter) to produce aligned nanofibres (fibre diameter ~500 nm).
- The electrospun mesh provides **tensile strength** (Young's modulus ~10–15 MPa, matching native submucosa). *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Cell Seeding on Outer Layer**
- Seed **smooth muscle cells (SMCs)** isolated from oesophageal biopsy onto the PCL mesh at **5 × 10⁵ cells/cm²** using **dynamic rotational seeding** (24 h on a rotating platform).
- SMCs align along the fibre direction due to **contact guidance**, mimicking native muscularis orientation. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Assembly of Bilayered Construct**
- The collagen hydrogel is cast into the lumen of the electrospun PCL tube.
- Co-culture maintained in **DMEM/F12 with EGF (10 ng/mL), KGF (20 ng/mL), and TGF-β1 (5 ng/mL)** for epithelial and muscular differentiation respectively. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Bioreactor Preconditioning (7–14 days)**
- The construct is mounted in a **peristaltic bioreactor**.
- Luminal flow of medium at **τw ≈ 0.5 dyn/cm²** promotes epithelial stratification.
- Cyclic radial stretching at **0.5 Hz, 10% strain** promotes SMC alignment and ECM deposition.
- Outcome: a mature, mechanically conditioned bilayered graft ready for implantation. *(1 mark)*

**Diagram (required, 1 mark):** Include a labelled cross-section showing inner hydrogel + epithelial cells, outer PCL mesh + SMCs.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a **labelled diagram**, describe the **decellularisation process** of a porcine oesophagus. List **three decellularisation reagents** and explain the **biochemical principle** behind each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Laplace's Law for a hollow cylinder**, derive the minimum wall thickness of an oesophageal substitute required to withstand a peak intraluminal pressure of $180 \text{ mmHg}$, given internal radius $r = 8 \text{ mm}$ and allowable wall tensile stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$. State your assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Decellularisation of Porcine Oesophagus**

**Process overview:**
- Porcine oesophagus is harvested under aseptic conditions, trimmed, and sliced into **~3 cm segments**.
- Subjected to a **multi-step detergent–enzymatic treatment** to remove cells while preserving ECM.

**Three decellularisation reagents & their principles:**

1. **Sodium Dodecyl Sulphate (SDS, 0.5–1%):** An **anionic detergent** that solubilises cytoplasmic and nuclear membranes by disrupting lipid bilayers and denaturing proteins. Effective for nuclear removal. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Triton X-100 (0.5–1%):** A **non-ionic detergent** that disrupts lipid–lipid and lipid–protein interactions while preserving ECM protein structure (collagen, elastin). *(1.5 marks)*
3. **DNase I + RNase (50 U/mL each):** **Hydrolytic enzymes** that cleave residual DNA/RNA fragments into nucleotides that diffuse out, reducing the DNA content to **< 50 ng/mg dry weight** (the minimum criterion per Crapo et al., 2011). *(1.5 marks)*

**Protocol (briefly):**
- Agitation at 120 rpm, 37 °C, in SDS for 4 h → rinse → Triton X-100 for 4 h → rinse → DNase/RNase for 3 h → extensive PBS washes.
- Final storage in **antibiotic/antimycotic solution** at 4 °C. *(1 mark)*

**Validation:** Histology (H&E — absence of nuclei), DAPI staining, DNA quantification, and mechanical testing. *(0.5 mark)*

**Diagram (1 mark):** Labelled flow showing harvested oesophagus → detergent bath 1 → rinse → detergent bath 2 → enzyme treatment → acellular scaffold.

**(b) Derivation of Minimum Wall Thickness**

**Assumptions:**
- Hollow cylindrical geometry.
- Thin-walled approximation valid ($h \ll r$).
- Uniform, isotropic material behaviour.
- Steady-state intraluminal pressure.
- Lamellar, defect-free wall.

**Step 1: Convert pressure to SI units**

$$P = 180 \text{ mmHg} \times \dfrac{101325}{760} = 23998.03 \text{ Pa} \approx 24.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: State Laplace's Law for a cylinder**

$$T = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot h}$$

**Step 3: Relate wall tension to tensile stress**

Wall tensile stress is force per unit cross-sectional area of the wall:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{T}{1} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot h}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange for minimum thickness**

$$h_{\min} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute values**

$$h_{\min} = \dfrac{23998.03 \times 0.008}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator and denominator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 191.98 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 7: Final calculation**

$$h_{\min} = \dfrac{191.98}{10^6} = 1.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{\min} \approx 0.192 \text{ mm} \approx 192 \text{ μm}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):**
This minimum thickness (**~200 μm**) is in remarkable agreement with the diffusion-limited thickness derived in Section 3.2 — confirming the **dual design constraint** that oesophageal walls must be **strong enough to resist burst** AND **thin enough to allow nutrient diffusion** in the early avascular phase. Designs satisfying both constraints typically fall in the **200–400 μm** range, with thicker regions requiring pre-vascularisation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Oesophageal Tissue Engineering (Module 2)**

- **Anatomy:** Four layers (Mucosa → Submucosa → Muscularis Propria → Adventitia). **No serosa** → poor suturing strength.
- **Clinical Need:** Cancer (7th globally), caustic injury, atresia (1/4000 births), strictures, achalasia.
- **Triad:** Cells (autologous keratinocytes + SMCs) + Scaffold (DHO/PCL/GelMA) + Signals (EGF, KGF, TGF-β, mechanical stimulation).
- **Ideal substitute specs:** Burst pressure **>200 mmHg**, $E \approx 0.5\text{–}3$ MPa, biodegradable in **8–12 weeks**, anti-stricture, pre-vascularised.
- **Key formulas (must memorise):**
  - Laplace: $T = Pr/(2h)$ → for burst strength.
  - Fick: $J = -D \cdot dC/dx$ → limits diffusion depth to **~200 μm** without vascularisation.
  - Wall shear stress: $\tau_w = 4\mu Q/(\pi r^3)$ → drives epithelial differentiation in bioreactors.
  - Degradation: $\ln(M_t) = \ln(M_0) - k_d t$.
- **Decellularisation reagents:** SDS (anionic, removes nuclei), Triton X-100 (non-ionic, preserves ECM), DNase/RNase (cleave nucleic acids). **Acceptance criterion:** < 50 ng DNA/mg dry weight.
- **Bioreactor:** Peristaltic pump + cyclic stretch (0.5 Hz, 10% strain) for 7–14 days pre-implant.
- **Frontier technologies:** 3D bioprinting with vascular channels, oesophageal organoids from iPSCs, and cell-sheet engineering (no scaffold).
- **Common pitfalls:** Using spherical Laplace formula, omitting units, forgetting assumptions in derivations, ignoring the no-serosa clinical reality.
- **Clinical translation status:** Mostly pre-clinical; SIS-ECM patches have reached limited human trials; 3D-bioprinted grafts remain investigational.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vascular Graft Tissue Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT754 - TISSUE ENGINEERING & BIOFABRICATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vascular Graft Tissue Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vascular Graft Tissue Engineering** is a specialized sub-discipline of regenerative medicine that applies the principles of tissue engineering to fabricate living, functional, and mechanically robust blood vessel substitutes capable of integrating, remodeling, and growing within the host circulatory system. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, it is defined as *"the interdisciplinary field that combines living cells, biomaterial scaffolds, biochemical cues, and biomechanical stimulation to construct neovessels that mimic the structural hierarchy, compliance, and biofunctionality of native arteries, veins, and capillaries."*

A *vascular graft* is a tubular conduit (typically **6 mm** diameter or less for coronary/critical applications) surgically implanted to bypass, replace, or reroute diseased blood vessels. The engineering objective is to overcome the limitations of autologous grafts (limited availability, donor site morbidity) and synthetic grafts (compliance mismatch, intimal hyperplasia, thrombosis, no growth potential) by creating **biohybrid living grafts**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Vascular grafts are categorized by internal diameter as:
> - **Large-diameter grafts (≥ 8 mm)** — ePTFE, Dacron clinically successful (e.g., aortic replacement).
> - **Small-diameter grafts (< 6 mm)** — THE central challenge of vascular TE. No clinically approved TE product yet for coronary bypass.
> - **Medium-diameter (6–8 mm)** — Intermediate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy city water pipeline (the **artery**) that has a corroded, leaking section. You cannot shut the entire city down, so you need a **replacement pipe**. A regular plastic pipe (synthetic graft like Dacron) can carry water, but it is **stiff**, **unresponsive** to pressure surges, and over time **calcium deposits** form on its inner wall, eventually clogging it. Worse, the plastic cannot repair a small scratch, cannot grow if the city expands, and is rejected by the body's "security guards" (immune cells).

**Vascular tissue engineering is like building a *living* pipe** — a tube whose wall is made of real biological cells (smooth muscle, endothelial) embedded in a natural protein scaffold (collagen/elastin), grown in a pulsating "training tank" (bioreactor) that teaches the cells to behave like a real vessel. When implanted, this living pipe **heals**, **remodels**, and **grows with the patient** — something no plastic pipe can ever do.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A native artery is a *composite laminate* — intima (endothelial monolayer), media (concentric layers of smooth muscle cells in elastin/collagen matrix), and adventitia (fibroblasts in collagen type I). Any successful TE graft must recapitulate this **tri-layer architecture**.

## 1.3 Standard Design Metrics (Quantitative Targets)

For a coronary artery graft, the gold-standard design specifications are:

| Parameter | Target Value | Native Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Internal diameter | **3–4 mm** | 3.0–4.0 mm |
| Burst pressure | **> 2000 mmHg** | ~3000 mmHg |
| Suture retention | **> 1 N** | ~1.5 N |
| Compliance | **5–15 %/100 mmHg** | 9–15 %/100 mmHg |
| Porosity | **50–100 µm** for cell infiltration | Layer-dependent |
| Patency (1 year) | **> 90 %** | Native ~99 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compliance** is the fractional diameter change per unit pressure change. It is the *single most important* mechanical parameter — a mismatch between graft and native vessel (ΔCompliance > 5 %/100 mmHg) causes **intimal hyperplasia** at the anastomosis (suture site), the primary cause of graft failure.

---

## 1.4 Cell Sources for Vascular TE

- **Autologous vascular cells** — SMCs from saphenous vein biopsy, ECs from same source.
- **Stem cells** — Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), adipose-derived stem cells (ADSCs), induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs).
- **Endothelial progenitor cells (EPCs)** — Circulating, can be isolated from peripheral blood, expand and form confluent luminal lining.

## 1.5 Scaffold Families

- **Natural polymers** — Collagen (type I/III), elastin, fibrin, chitosan, silk fibroin, hyaluronic acid, decellularized ECM.
- **Synthetic biodegradable polymers** — Polyglycolic acid (PGA), polylactic acid (PLA), polycaprolactone (PCL), poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA), poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG).
- **Composite/hybrid scaffolds** — Combining natural compliance with synthetic strength (e.g., PGA + collagen, PCL + elastin).
- **Decellularized native vessels** — Allogenic/xenogenic vessels stripped of cellular antigens but retaining intact ECM architecture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Vascular graft tissue engineering is executed through **five sequential pillars**. Each must be satisfied to obtain a functional graft.

### Pillar 1: Scaffold Fabrication (Tubular Geometry)
- The scaffold provides a 3D template that defines the **macroscopic tubular shape**, mechanical strength, and microscopic cues for cell adhesion.
- Fabrication routes:
  * **Electrospinning** — Produces nano-/microfibrous non-woven meshes; tunable fiber diameter, alignment, porosity.
  * **Phase separation / freeze-drying** — Creates interconnected porous foams.
  * **Wet spinning / extrusion** — Produces aligned fiber bundles.
  * **3D bioprinting** — Layer-by-layer deposition of bioinks (cell-laden hydrogels).
  * **Decellularization** — Chemical (SDS, Triton X-100), enzymatic (trypsin, DNase), or physical (freeze-thaw) removal of cells from native vessels.

### Pillar 2: Cell Seeding & Culturing
- **Static seeding** — Cells dripped onto scaffold; poor penetration.
- **Dynamic (rotational/spinner flask) seeding** — Improves uniformity.
- **Perfusion seeding** — Cells flowed through lumen under pressure; best penetration and alignment.
- Typical cell densities: **1–5 × 10⁶ cells/cm²** for endothelialization; **5–10 × 10⁶ cells/mL** for SMC seeding.

### Pillar 3: Bioreactor Conditioning (Mechanical Stimulation)
- The most critical step for small-diameter grafts. Mimics the **pulsatile, laminar shear** environment of the circulatory system.
- Parameters:
  * **Pulsatile pressure**: 80/120 mmHg (coronary), with sinusoidal/physiological waveform.
  * **Shear stress (τ)**: 10–15 dyne/cm² for arterial endothelium.
  * **Frequency**: ~1 Hz (60 bpm).
  * **Duration**: 4–8 weeks maturation.
- Outcomes of conditioning: ECM production ↑, SMCs align circumferentially, ECs align with flow, burst strength ↑ 5–10×.

### Pillar 4: Endothelialization (Lumen Lining)
- The luminal surface is seeded with ECs/EPCs to provide an **antithrombogenic, anti-inflammatory interface**.
- Without endothelium, collagen exposure activates **platelets → thrombosis**, the #1 cause of early graft failure.
- ECs respond to shear by aligning **longitudinally** (parallel to flow), upregulating **eNOS**, and producing **heparan sulfate** (anticoagulant).

### Pillar 5: Implantation & In Vivo Remodeling
- Graft is anastomosed (sutured) end-to-side or end-to-end to host vessel.
- Host cells (fibroblasts, SMCs, ECs) infiltrate; the graft is gradually **replaced by host tissue** while the scaffold biodegrades.
- Inflammatory response must be controlled — too much leads to stenosis; too little leads to aneurysmal dilation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{4\mu Q}{\pi r^{3}}$ | Wall shear stress (Poiseuille) | dyne/cm² ; μ = viscosity, Q = flow, r = radius |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress from pressure drop | dyne/cm² ; for straight tube |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (laminar threshold Re < 2100) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C = \dfrac{\Delta D / D_{0}}{\Delta P} \times 100$ | Compliance | % / 100 mmHg |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{h}$ | Hoop stress (Laplace's law) | Pa or dyne/cm² ; h = wall thickness |
| 6 | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS} \cdot h}{r}$ | Theoretical burst pressure | mmHg or kPa |
| 7 | $h_{0} = h_{s} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{s}}{\rho_{t}}$ | Wall thickness matching | ratio of synthetic to tissue density |
| 8 | $WSS = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{wall}$ | Shear stress at wall | dyne/cm² |
| 9 | $t_{degr} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{deg}}$ | Half-life of biodegradable scaffold | days; $k_{deg}$ = degradation rate constant |
| 10 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | cm²/s (blood ≈ 0.04 cm²/s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical evaluation rules for the exam:**
> 1. Hoop stress $\sigma$ in a thin-walled tube is **TWICE** the longitudinal stress — vessels rupture **circumferentially** first.
> 2. Wall shear stress for **arteries**: 10–70 dyne/cm²; for **veins**: 1–6 dyne/cm². ECs require ≥ 10 dyne/cm² to maintain quiescence.
> 3. A drop in shear stress below 4 dyne/cm² (or rise above 70 dyne/cm²) triggers EC apoptosis and atherogenesis.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG)** — 400,000+ procedures/year in USA alone; ~30 % lack suitable autologous vein (saphenous).
- **Peripheral arterial disease (PAD)** — Below-knee bypass for diabetic patients; no FDA-approved TE small-diameter graft yet.
- **Pediatric congenital cardiac surgery** — Living grafts that *grow with the child* are the holy grail; existing synthetic grafts require multiple re-operations.
- **Hemodialysis access (AV graft)** — AV-shunt grafts fail at ~50 % within 2 years due to intimal hyperplasia at venous anastomosis.
- **In vitro disease modeling** — TE vascular rings used in pharmacological testing of vasoactive drugs.

---

## 2.4 Failure Modes (KTU-favorite topic)

| Mode | Cause | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|
| **Acute thrombosis** | No/loss of endothelium; collagen exposure | < 1 month |
| **Intimal hyperplasia** | Compliance mismatch; disturbed flow at anastomosis | 1–24 months |
| **Aneurysmal dilation** | Incomplete ECM deposition; weak scaffold | > 2 years |
| **Calcification** | Phospholipid-rich necrotic cores; osteogenic differentiation | > 3 years |
| **Infection** | Biofilm on synthetic material | Any time |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Wall Shear Stress (Poiseuille Flow)

Consider steady, laminar, Newtonian flow of blood through a rigid cylindrical tube of radius $r$ and length $L$. The Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates reduces to the Hagen–Poiseuille equation.

**Step 1:** The velocity profile for fully developed laminar flow in a circular tube is parabolic:

$$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\,(R^{2} - r^{2})$$

**Step 2:** The volumetric flow rate (Hagen–Poiseuille) is obtained by integrating the velocity profile over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v(r)\, 2\pi r\, dr$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the velocity expression and integrate:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\,(R^{2} - r^{2})\, 2\pi r\, dr$$

**Step 4:** Factor constants outside the integral:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^{2} r - r^{3})\, dr$$

**Step 5:** Perform the integration term by term:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the upper limit $r = R$ and lower limit $r = 0$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( \frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} \right) = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

**Step 7:** The classical Hagen–Poiseuille law is therefore:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P\, R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 8:** Differentiate the velocity profile to obtain the shear rate at the wall ($r = R$):

$$\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = -\left. \frac{dv}{dr} \right|_{r=R} = \frac{\Delta P\, R}{2 \mu L}$$

**Step 9:** Wall shear stress is viscosity × shear rate:

$$\tau_{wall} = \mu \dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{\Delta P\, R}{2 L}$$

**Step 10:** Eliminate $\Delta P$ by using the Hagen–Poiseuille law solved for $\Delta P/L$:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8 \mu Q}{\pi R^{4}}$$

**Step 11:** Substitute back into the wall shear stress equation:

$$\tau_{wall} = \frac{R}{2} \cdot \frac{8 \mu Q}{\pi R^{4}} = \frac{4 \mu Q}{\pi R^{3}}$$

This is the **canonical formula** used in vascular TE for estimating luminal shear stress on endothelial cells — typically **10–15 dyne/cm²** for a healthy coronary artery. If shear falls below **4 dyne/cm²** at the anastomosis, ECs lose their atheroprotective phenotype.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Burst Pressure from Hoop Stress (Laplace's Law)

**Step 1:** For a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel with internal pressure $P$, radius $R$, and wall thickness $h$, equilibrium of forces on a half-cylinder of length $L$ gives:

$$P \cdot (2R \cdot L) = 2 \cdot \sigma_{hoop} \cdot h \cdot L$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the hoop stress:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot R}{h}$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging for the burst pressure $P_{burst}$, where the wall fails at the ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{UTS}$:

$$P_{burst} = \frac{\sigma_{UTS} \cdot h}{R}$$

**Step 4:** **Numerical example** — For a TE graft with $\sigma_{UTS} = 1.5 \,\text{MPa}$, $h = 0.5 \,\text{mm}$, $R = 1.5 \,\text{mm}$:

$$P_{burst} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa})\,(0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m})}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}} = 5.0 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to mmHg (1 mmHg = 133.32 Pa):

$$P_{burst} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{5}}{133.32} \approx 3750 \,\text{mmHg}$$

This comfortably exceeds the physiological systolic pressure (120 mmHg) by **> 30×**, providing a **safety factor of ~30** — a standard design target for vascular grafts.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Compliance Definition & Mismatch Penalty

**Step 1:** The compliance $C$ of a vessel is defined as the fractional change in internal diameter per unit pressure change:

$$C = \frac{D_{sys} - D_{dia}}{D_{dia} \cdot (P_{sys} - P_{dia})} \times 100 \quad \left[\frac{\%}{100\,\text{mmHg}}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Define the compliance mismatch as:

$$\Delta C = \left\vert C_{graft} - C_{native} \right\vert$$

**Step 3:** A common empirical correlation (Ballyk et al., 1998) relates the intimal hyperplasia thickness $H$ to the compliance mismatch:

$$H \propto \frac{\Delta C}{C_{native}}$$

**Step 4:** For $\Delta C > 5\, \%/100\,\text{mmHg}$, flow separation occurs at the distal anastomosis, leading to **low wall shear stress regions (τ < 4 dyne/cm²)**. This triggers EC apoptosis, smooth muscle migration, and ultimately **intimal hyperplasia** — the dominant cause of mid-term graft failure.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Scaffold Material Trade-offs

| Material | Tensile Strength | Compliance | Degradation (months) | Thrombogenicity | Cell Affinity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dacron (PET)** | High (~170 MPa) | Low | Non-degradable | Moderate (requires pre-clotting) | Low |
| **ePTFE (Gore-Tex)** | High (~14 MPa) | Low | Non-degradable | Low (when ≤ 30 µm pores) | Low |
| **PGA** | Moderate | Moderate | 2–4 | Low | High |
| **PLA** | Moderate | Low | 12–24 | Low | Moderate |
| **PCL** | Low–Moderate | Moderate | 24–48 | Very low | Moderate |
| **PLGA** | Tunable | Tunable | 1–6 (tunable) | Low | Tunable |
| **Collagen type I** | Low (~5 MPa dry) | High | 1–3 (in vivo) | High (unless crosslinked) | Excellent |
| **Elastin** | Moderate | Very High | Slow | Low | Good |
| **Silk fibroin** | High (~500 MPa) | Moderate | > 12 | Low | Good |
| **Decellularized ECM** | Native (~1.5 MPa) | Native-like | Slow (years) | Low | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid strategy:** A typical TE vascular graft uses **PGA scaffold** (for mechanical strength) wrapped in a **collagen gel** (for cell encapsulation and compliance), then conditioned in a **pulsatile bioreactor**. This is the basis of the landmark **L'Heureux et al. (1998) "sheet-based" graft** and the Niklason group (1999) PGA-based graft.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Shear Stress in a Coronary Graft

A tissue-engineered coronary graft has internal radius $R = 1.5\,\text{mm}$, blood flow $Q = 250\,\text{mL/min}$, and blood viscosity $\mu = 3.5\,\text{cP} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$.

**Step 1:** Convert $Q$ to SI units:

$$Q = 250 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{3} / 60\,\text{s} = 4.17 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the wall shear stress formula:

$$\tau = \frac{4 \mu Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \frac{4 \cdot (3.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (4.17 \times 10^{-6})}{\pi \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator and denominator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 5.84 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = \pi \cdot 3.375 \times 10^{-9} = 1.06 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 4:** Divide:

$$\tau = \frac{5.84 \times 10^{-8}}{1.06 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 5.51\,\text{Pa} = 55.1\,\text{dyne/cm}^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: $\tau = 55.1$ dyne/cm² falls within the **arterial atheroprotective range (10–70 dyne/cm²)** — the bioreactor operating condition is correctly tuned. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Native Artery vs. TE Graft — Tri-Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NativeArtery["NATIVE ARTERY - Tri Layer Architecture"]
        L1["LUMEN - Blood Flow Contact"]
        L2["LAYER 1 - TUNICA INTIMA - Endothelial monolayer on basal lamina"]
        L3["LAYER 2 - TUNICA MEDIA - Circumferential smooth muscle in elastin and collagen matrix"]
        L4["LAYER 3 - TUNICA ADVENTITIA - Longitudinal fibroblasts in collagen type I"]
        L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
    end

    subgraph TEGraft["TE GRAFT - Engineered Analog"]
        E1["LUMEN - Endothelialized Surface"]
        E2["SCAFFOLD 1 - PGA or PLA or PCL fiber mesh"]
        E3["SCAFFOLD 2 - Collagen or fibrin hydrogel with SMCs"]
        E4["SCAFFOLD 3 - Collagen type I outer wrap with fibroblasts"]
        E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4
    end

    NativeArtery -.Mimics.-> TEGraft
```

## 4.2 Vascular Graft TE Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STEP 1 - SCAFFOLD FABRICATION"] --> A1["Electrospinning of PCL or PLA fibers"]
    A --> A2["Decellularization of native vessel SDS Triton"]
    A --> A3["3D Bioprinting of tubular construct"]
    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B

    B["STEP 2 - CELL EXPANSION"] --> B1["SMC from saphenous vein biopsy"]
    B --> B2["EC from same donor"]
    B --> B3["MSC or iPSC derived cells"]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C

    C["STEP 3 - CELL SEEDING"] --> C1["Dynamic perfusion seeding of SMCs into scaffold wall"]
    C --> C2["Static or rotational seeding of ECs on luminal surface"]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D

    D["STEP 4 - BIOREACTOR CONDITIONING"] --> D1["Pulsatile flow 80 over 120 mmHg 1 Hz"]
    D --> D2["Wall shear 10 to 15 dyne per cm squared"]
    D --> D3["Duration 4 to 8 weeks in DMEM plus TGF beta plus ascorbic acid"]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    E["STEP 5 - MATURE GRAFT QUALITY CONTROL"] --> E1["Burst pressure test minimum 2000 mmHg"]
    E --> E2["Suture retention test minimum 1 N"]
    E --> E3["Endothelial confluence and eNOS expression"]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F["STEP 6 - SURGICAL IMPLANTATION"] --> F1["End to side anastomosis to host vessel"]
    F --> F2["In vivo remodeling - host cell infiltration"]
    F --> F3["Long term monitoring - patency and compliance"]
```

## 4.3 Bioreactor Pulsatile Conditioning System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BioreactorSystem["PULSATILE BIOREACTOR SYSTEM"]
        P["Peristaltic or piston pump - generates pulsatile flow 1 Hz"]
        R["Reservoir - culture medium DMEM with 10 percent FBS and growth factors"]
        C["Compliance chamber - simulates vascular compliance"]
        G["TE GRAFT - mounted in chamber under sterile conditions"]
        T["Pressure transducer - real time monitoring of 80 over 120 mmHg"]
        F["Flow meter - measures Q and computes wall shear stress"]
        O["Gas exchange - O2 21 percent and CO2 5 percent for cell metabolism"]

        P -->|pulsatile flow| G
        R --> P
        C --> G
        G -->|effluent| R
        T -->|feedback| P
        F -->|data logging| T
        O --> R
    end
```

## 4.4 Failure Cascade Flow — Compliance Mismatch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0["Stiff synthetic graft with low compliance"] --> S1["Compliance mismatch at anastomosis greater than 5 percent per 100 mmHg"]
    S1 --> S2["Flow separation and recirculation zone downstream of suture"]
    S2 --> S3["Low wall shear stress below 4 dyne per cm squared"]
    S3 --> S4["Endothelial cell apoptosis and loss of eNOS expression"]
    S4 --> S5["Platelet adhesion and inflammatory cell infiltration"]
    S5 --> S6["Smooth muscle cell migration and proliferation into intima"]
    S6 --> S7["INTIMAL HYPERPLASIA - luminal narrowing"]
    S7 --> S8["Graft stenosis and occlusion - clinical failure"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretive Note on Diagram 4.4:** This is the *single most-tested cascade* in vascular TE. The exam often asks students to identify the *rate-limiting step* (Step S2 — flow separation, which is the hemodynamic initiator).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define vascular graft tissue engineering. Why is the small-diameter graft (< 6 mm) problem considered unsolved by conventional synthetic materials?**

**Model Answer:**

Vascular graft tissue engineering is the application of tissue-engineering principles — cells, scaffolds, bioreactors, and signaling cues — to fabricate living blood vessel substitutes that can integrate, remodel, and grow within the host.

The small-diameter graft problem remains unsolved because synthetic polymers (Dacron, ePTFE) used in large-diameter grafts (> 8 mm) fail catastrophically when used in small diameters due to:
- **Compliance mismatch** with native arteries → flow disturbance → intimal hyperplasia.
- **Thrombogenicity** of the luminal surface (no endothelial lining) → acute occlusion.
- **Inability to grow or remodel** → particularly problematic in pediatric applications.
- **Lack of a functional, antithrombogenic endothelium** that can be sustained under high-velocity, high-shear arterial flow.

Therefore, a *living, autologous, biofunctional* vessel — built via tissue engineering — is the most promising solution.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the three layers of a native artery and their corresponding engineered analogs in a tissue-engineered vascular graft (TEVG).**

**Model Answer:**

| Native Layer | Composition | TEVG Analog |
|---|---|---|
| **Tunica intima** | Endothelial cells (ECs) on basal lamina | ECs or EPCs seeded on luminal surface |
| **Tunica media** | Smooth muscle cells (SMCs) in elastin/collagen matrix | SMCs in collagen or fibrin gel + PGA/PCL scaffold |
| **Tunica adventitia** | Fibroblasts in type I collagen | Fibroblasts in collagen type I wrap; outer PGA mesh |

The tri-layer design recapitulates the *structural hierarchy* and *mechanical anisotropy* of native vessels — most importantly, the **circumferential alignment of medial SMCs** that provides **burst strength**, and the **longitudinal alignment of intimal ECs** that provides **antithrombogenicity**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the design criteria for an ideal small-diameter vascular graft. Include mechanical, biological, and hemocompatibility requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the pulsatile bioreactor system used for vascular graft maturation. Explain the role of mechanical stimulation in ECM production and SMC alignment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Design Criteria for Small-Diameter Vascular Graft**

The design of a small-diameter TEVG must satisfy the following *non-negotiable* criteria, derived from native vessel physiology and clinical failure modes:

1. **Mechanical Criteria:**
   - **Burst pressure** ≥ 2000 mmHg (native ~ 3000 mmHg) — to survive hypertensive events.
   - **Suture retention strength** ≥ 1 N — so the graft does not tear at the anastomosis.
   - **Compliance** of 5–15 %/100 mmHg — to match native coronary artery (~ 9 %/100 mmHg) and prevent intimal hyperplasia.
   - **Elastic modulus** comparable to native artery — provides physiological Windkessel effect.
   - **Fatigue resistance** — must withstand ~ 40 million cycles/year.

2. **Biological Criteria:**
   - **Biocompatibility** — non-toxic, non-immunogenic.
   - **Biodegradability** — controlled degradation matching new ECM deposition rate ($t_{degr} \sim$ 2–6 months).
   - **Cell adhesion and infiltration** — surface chemistry supporting cell attachment.
   - **Remodeling capacity** — must allow host cell infiltration and matrix turnover.
   - **Growth potential** — particularly critical for pediatric applications.

3. **Hemocompatibility Criteria:**
   - **Confluent, quiescent endothelial lining** on the lumen — secretes NO, heparan sulfate, thrombomodulin.
   - **Antithrombogenic surface** — minimal platelet activation.
   - **Smooth luminal surface** — no flow disturbance.
   - **Wall shear stress in physiological range** (10–15 dyne/cm²) to maintain EC phenotype.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part a):** Students commonly forget the **fatigue** criterion. A graft that withstands one static burst test may fail after millions of cyclic loading. Always mention cyclic fatigue in mechanical criteria.

---

**(b) Pulsatile Bioreactor System and Mechanical Stimulation**

A **pulsatile bioreactor** is a closed-loop flow system that mimics the *in vivo* hemodynamic environment of the arterial circulation. Its main components are:

1. **Pulsatile pump** — peristaltic or piston-driven; produces sinusoidal pressure waveform.
2. **Culture medium reservoir** — DMEM + 10% FBS + TGF-β + ascorbic acid (cofactor for collagen crosslinking).
3. **Sterile graft chamber** — graft mounted on mandrels, sealed at both ends.
4. **Compliance chamber / Windkessel element** — simulates arterial compliance.
5. **Pressure & flow transducers** — real-time feedback.
6. **Gas exchange unit** — 21 % O₂, 5 % CO₂, 37 °C.

**Operating conditions:**
- Pressure: **80/120 mmHg** (coronary equivalent).
- Frequency: **1 Hz** (60 bpm).
- Shear stress: **10–15 dyne/cm²** (atheroprotective).
- Duration: **4–8 weeks**.

**Role of Mechanical Stimulation:**

1. **ECM Production ↑** — Cyclic strain upregulates **collagen type I, elastin, and fibrillin** synthesis in SMCs via **MAPK/ERK** and **TGF-β** pathways. Ascorbic acid is required as a cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase (collagen crosslinking).
2. **SMC Alignment** — SMCs align **circumferentially** (perpendicular to the axial strain direction), reproducing the native medial architecture. This alignment provides the graft's **load-bearing capacity**.
3. **Enhanced Burst Strength** — A typical static-cultured PGA graft has burst pressure ~ 200–400 mmHg. After 8 weeks of pulsatile conditioning, it reaches **> 2000 mmHg**.
4. **Endothelial Phenotype Maintenance** — Shear stress aligns ECs longitudinally, upregulates **eNOS** and **VE-cadherin**, and suppresses **VCAM-1/ICAM-1**, conferring an antithrombogenic, anti-inflammatory surface.
5. **Improved Compliance** — Pulsatile strain promotes organized elastin deposition, allowing elastic recoil and matching native compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** Students often state "pulsatile flow increases ECM" without specifying **which pathways** (e.g., TGF-β / MAPK / integrin-mediated mechanotransduction). Always tie *molecular mechanism* to *macroscopic outcome* to score full marks.

---

### CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the "compliance mismatch" problem in vascular grafts. Derive the relationship between compliance and intimal hyperplasia. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of endothelial cells (ECs) in vascular graft hemocompatibility. What strategies are used to achieve luminal endothelialization in TEVGs? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Compliance Mismatch & Intimal Hyperplasia**

**Definition:** Compliance ($C$) is the fractional change in vessel diameter per unit pressure change:

$$C = \frac{\Delta D / D_{0}}{\Delta P} \times 100 \quad \left[\frac{\%}{100\,\text{mmHg}}\right]$$

A native coronary artery has $C \approx 9$–$15\, \%/100\,\text{mmHg}$. A synthetic ePTFE graft has $C \approx 1$–$2\, \%/100\,\text{mmHg}$ — i.e., it is **~ 10× stiffer** than the native vessel.

**The Mismatch Problem:**

When a stiff graft is sutured to a compliant native artery, the **diameter does not change uniformly** with the cardiac pulse. At the anastomosis (suture line), this produces a **compliance step** that creates a **flow separation zone** on the downstream (distal) side. The hemodynamic consequences are:

1. **Low wall shear stress** ($\tau < 4$ dyne/cm²) in the separation zone.
2. **Oscillatory shear index (OSI) > 0.5** — flow reversal during diastole.
3. **Loss of atheroprotective phenotype** of ECs (downregulation of eNOS, KLF-2, KLF-4; upregulation of VCAM-1, MCP-1).
4. **Recruitment of monocytes** → secretion of PDGF, TGF-β.
5. **Smooth muscle cell migration** from media to intima, proliferation, ECM deposition.
6. **Intimal thickening** at the anastomosis, narrowing the lumen.

**Empirical Relationship (Ballyk et al., 1998):**

The intimal hyperplasia thickness $H$ at the anastomosis is empirically proportional to the **compliance mismatch ratio**:

$$H \propto \left( \frac{C_{native} - C_{graft}}{C_{native}} \right)$$

A mismatch greater than **5 %/100 mmHg** is the clinical threshold above which mid-term patency drops sharply.

---

**(b) Role of Endothelial Cells & Strategies for Endothelialization**

**Role of ECs in Hemocompatibility:**

The endothelium is the body's **natural anticoagulant surface**. A healthy, confluent endothelial monolayer prevents thrombosis by:

- **Producing NO (nitric oxide)** via **eNOS** — vasodilator, inhibits platelet aggregation, suppresses SMC proliferation.
- **Producing prostacyclin (PGI₂)** — anti-platelet, vasodilator.
- **Expressing thrombomodulin** — binds thrombin, switches its substrate from fibrinogen to protein C → activated protein C (APC) inhibits factors Va and VIIIa.
- **Expressing heparan sulfate** on the glycocalyx — binds antithrombin III (ATIII), inactivates thrombin.
- **Expressing tissue factor pathway inhibitor (TFPI)** — blocks extrinsic coagulation.
- **Tight junctions (VE-cadherin, claudins)** — prevent exposure of sub-endothelial collagen to platelets.

**Strategies for Luminal Endothelialization in TEVGs:**

1. **Pre-seeding of autologous ECs** — Cells harvested from saphenous vein, expanded *in vitro*, and seeded onto the luminal surface; cultured under shear to mature.
2. **Endothelial progenitor cell (EPC) capture** — Antibodies (e.g., anti-CD34) immobilized on the luminal surface capture circulating EPCs *in situ* after implantation. The **Genous™ bioengineered stent** uses this approach.
3. **iPSC-derived ECs** — Patient-specific iPSCs differentiated to ECs; eliminates immunogenicity but raises tumorigenicity concerns.
4. **Shear-preconditioning** — Rotating or perfusion seeding aligns ECs longitudinally, induces physiological phenotype.
5. **Surface modification** — Coating lumen with **RGD peptides**, **REDV sequence** (EC-specific adhesion), **elastin-derived sequences**, or **heparin** to promote EC adhesion and reduce platelet fouling.
6. **In situ endothelialization via stents/transluminal seeding** — Pre-seeded stents placed in the lumen deliver ECs at the time of implantation.
7. **Magnetic cell guidance** — ECs labeled with superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) and aligned on the lumen using an external magnetic field.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** Many students list only "seed ECs" without explaining the *anticoagulation mechanism*. The question tests both **biology** (molecules produced) and **engineering** (how to achieve it). A complete answer must cover **molecular** + **biomaterial** + **bioreactor** strategies.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Master Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in Vascular TE Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "burst pressure" and "suture retention"** — they are *different* mechanical tests; burst pressure is radial, suture retention is axial.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4–70 dyne/cm² EC shear window** — students often give a single value (e.g., "10 dyne/cm²") instead of a range.
> 3. **Writing "bioreactor helps cells grow"** — too vague. Always specify *which* cells, *which* stimulus, *which* signaling pathway, *which* outcome.
> 4. **Calling Dacron/ePTFE "biocompatible" without caveats** — they are bio*inert* but require pre-clotting; they are not "tissue-engineered."
> 5. **Missing the circumferential vs longitudinal alignment distinction** — SMCs align *circumferentially*; ECs align *longitudinally* (with flow). Reversing this loses marks.
> 6. **No mention of compliance mismatch leading to intimal hyperplasia** — the most tested clinical complication.
> 7. **Treating "scaffold" and "matrix" as synonyms** — scaffold is the *initial* 3D template (often synthetic, biodegradable); matrix is the *deposited* ECM (biological, host-derived). This is a key KTU distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Vascular TE aims to build a **living, autologous** graft that integrates, remodels, and grows.
- ✅ The **small-diameter (< 6 mm) graft problem** is the central unmet clinical need (no FDA-approved TEVG yet for coronary bypass).
- ✅ The native artery has **three layers** — intima (EC), media (SMC in elastin/collagen), adventitia (fibroblast in collagen I) — and a TEVG must mimic all three.
- ✅ The **tissue-engineering triad** is: **cells + scaffold + bioreactor (signaling)**.
- ✅ Cell sources: **autologous SMCs & ECs**, **MSCs**, **ADSCs**, **iPSCs**, **EPCs**.
- ✅ Scaffolds: **natural** (collagen, elastin, fibrin, silk, decellularized ECM) vs **synthetic** (PGA, PLA, PCL, PLGA); **composites** are usually best.
- ✅ **Burst pressure** must be ≥ 2000 mmHg; **suture retention** ≥ 1 N; **compliance** 5–15 %/100 mmHg.
- ✅ Wall shear stress for healthy arterial ECs: **10–70 dyne/cm²**; **atherogenic** if τ < 4 or τ > 70 dyne/cm².
- ✅ **Hoop stress** $\sigma = PR/h$ is **twice** the longitudinal stress — vessels rupture circumferentially.
- ✅ **Pulsatile bioreactor conditioning** (4–8 weeks, 1 Hz, 80/120 mmHg, 10–15 dyne/cm²) is essential for small-diameter TEVG maturation.
- ✅ **Compliance mismatch** is the #1 cause of **intimal hyperplasia** at the anastomosis — keep ΔC < 5 %/100 mmHg.
- ✅ **Endothelialization** of the lumen is critical — it provides NO, PGI₂, thrombomodulin, and heparan sulfate to prevent thrombosis.
- ✅ **SMCs align circumferentially** (load-bearing); **ECs align longitudinally** (antithrombogenic) — these orientations are *opposite* and *non-interchangeable*.
- ✅ **TGF-β** and **ascorbic acid** are the two key biochemical additives that drive collagen and elastin production in bioreactor conditioning.
- ✅ **Decellularized native vessels** (e.g., HuSH graft, SynerGraft) preserve native ECM architecture but require thorough antigen removal.
- ✅ **L'Heureux's "sheet wrapping" technique** and **Niklason's PGA-based bioreactor graft** are landmark TEVG methods frequently cited in KTU questions.
- ✅ **Clinical translation challenges**: scale-up, sterilization, shelf-life, regulatory pathway, immunogenicity, and long-term patency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cardiac Tissue Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT754 - TISSUE ENGINEERING & BIOFABRICATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cardiac Tissue Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Cardiac Tissue Engineering

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cardiac Tissue Engineering (CTE)** is a specialised branch of regenerative medicine and tissue engineering that aims to construct functional biological substitutes for diseased or damaged myocardium (heart muscle tissue). It integrates principles of cell biology, biomaterials science, developmental biology, and biomechanics to fabricate **3D functional cardiac patches, in vitro cardiac tissue models, and injectable hydrogels** capable of restoring the electromechanical coupling, contractile function, and vascular integration of native heart tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Cardiac Tissue Engineering is the application of life sciences and engineering principles toward the development of biological substitutes that restore, maintain, or improve cardiac function, involving the triad of **(1) cells, (2) scaffolds/biomaterials, and (3) bioactive signals (mechanical/electrical/biochemical).**

### 1.2 Why is Cardiac Tissue Engineering Critical?

Cardiovascular Diseases (CVDs) remain the **leading cause of death globally**, accounting for approximately **17.9 million deaths per year** (WHO). In India alone, CVDs account for nearly **28% of all deaths**. The adult human heart has a regenerative capacity of less than **1% per year**, meaning that lost cardiomyocytes (heart muscle cells) after a myocardial infarction (heart attack) are replaced by non-contractile fibrotic scar tissue, leading to heart failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Clinical Challenge:** Approximately **1 billion cardiomyocytes** are lost during a typical myocardial infarction. The remaining viable myocardium cannot regenerate this volume, leading to adverse remodelling and eventual congestive heart failure.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human heart as a **mechanical water pump** with billions of tiny "fingers" (cardiomyocytes) that squeeze in synchrony to push blood out. After a heart attack, an entire region of these "fingers" dies and is replaced by a **rigid scar patch** (like cement filling a hole in the pump's wall). This rigid patch cannot squeeze, so the pump's efficiency drops.

**Cardiac Tissue Engineering** is like trying to **grow a new living, flexible, muscular patch** in a laboratory dish — one that can beat synchronously, conduct electrical impulses, and integrate seamlessly with the surrounding healthy tissue. Think of it as **"growing a replacement engine piston" for the heart pump** — except it is biological, self-beating, and electrically wired into the existing circuitry.

### 1.4 The Triad of Cardiac Tissue Engineering

| Component | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Cells (Seed)** | Cardiomyocytes or progenitors | iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs), ESC-CMs, MSCs, cardiac progenitor cells |
| **Scaffold (Framework)** | 3D matrix mimicking cardiac ECM | Collagen, fibrin, decellularised ECM, GelMA, PLGA, PEG hydrogels |
| **Signals (Stimuli)** | Biochemical, mechanical, electrical cues | TGF-β, IGF-1, cyclic stretch (1 Hz), electrical pacing (1-5 V/cm, 1-5 Hz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A successful cardiac construct must satisfy the **"4C Principle"** — **Contractility, Conduction, Compliance, and Connection (vascularisation).** Any engineered tissue failing one of these criteria will not function as a true myocardium replacement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Native Cardiac Tissue Architecture — The Design Blueprint

Before engineering, we must understand the native structure. The myocardium is a **highly organised, anisotropic, multilayered tissue** with the following hierarchical architecture:

1. **Cellular Level:** Cardiomyocytes are rod-shaped cells (~10–20 µm wide, ~50–100 µm long) aligned in parallel bundles.
2. **Tissue Level:** These bundles are arranged in sheets with rotation angles of ~120° between layers, enabling the characteristic twisting motion of the heart.
3. **Extracellular Matrix (ECM):** Composed primarily of **Collagen Type I (~85%) and Type III (~11%)**, elastin, fibronectin, laminin, and proteoglycans. ECM provides mechanical support and transmits contractile force.
4. **Vascular Network:** A dense capillary network with an **inter-capillary distance of ~20 µm** ensures oxygen diffusion.
5. **Electrical Network:** Gap junctions (primarily **Connexin-43**) couple cardiomyocytes electrically for synchronous contraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Parameter:** The maximum oxygen diffusion distance in cardiac tissue is **~200 µm** beyond which hypoxia and cell death occur. Any engineered cardiac construct thicker than this **must** have an internal vascular network.

### 2.2 Cell Sources for Cardiac Tissue Engineering

| Cell Type | Advantages | Disadvantages | Differentiation Yield |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Adult Cardiomyocytes** | Fully mature, functional | Cannot proliferate, scarce donor supply | N/A (direct isolation) |
| **Embryonic Stem Cells (ESCs)** | Pluripotent, high proliferation | Ethical concerns, teratoma risk | ~60-80% |
| **Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs)** | Patient-specific, autologous possible | Genomic instability, tumour risk | ~70-90% with small molecules |
| **Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs)** | Immunoprivileged, paracrine signalling | Limited cardiomyogenic differentiation | Paracrine action dominant |
| **Cardiac Progenitor Cells (CPCs)** | Committed to cardiac lineage | Low abundance, isolation difficulty | ~30-50% |

### 2.3 Biomaterials / Scaffolds for Cardiac Tissue Engineering

#### 2.3.1 Natural Biomaterials
- **Collagen Type I:** Most abundant cardiac ECM protein; biodegradable, biocompatible, supports cell adhesion via **integrin-binding RGD motifs**.
- **Fibrin:** Derived from fibrinogen; used in **"biowire"** and **"Engineered Heart Muscle (EHM)"** platforms; tunable degradation via aprotinin.
- **Decellularised Cardiac ECM:** Native composition preserved; immunogenic components (e.g., **α-Gal epitopes**) removed.
- **Gelatin Methacryloyl (GelMA):** Photocrosslinkable, tunable stiffness (1–30 kPa).

#### 2.3.2 Synthetic Biomaterials
- **Poly(glycerol sebacate) (PGS):** Elastomeric, biocompatible, biodegradable; Young's modulus tunable to match cardiac tissue (~0.02–0.5 MPa).
- **Poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG):** Bio-inert, can be functionalised with adhesive peptides.
- **PCL/PLGA blends:** Used for 3D printed cardiac patches with controlled degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffness Matching:** The ideal cardiac scaffold should have a **Young's Modulus in the range of 10–50 kPa** (matching native myocardium stiffness of ~10–20 kPa in diastole). Stiffer scaffolds (>100 kPa) lead to pathological cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, while overly soft substrates (<1 kPa) fail to provide mechanical integrity.

### 2.4 Biophysical Stimulation — The "Fourth Pillar" of Cardiac TE

Mature cardiomyocyte phenotype is **NOT achieved in static culture**. Active biophysical stimulation is mandatory:

| Stimulus Type | Parameters | Biological Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Cyclic Stretch)** | 1 Hz, 5–20% strain | Sarcomere alignment, hypertrophy, force generation |
| **Electrical Pacing** | 1–5 V/cm, 1–5 Hz, bipolar | Connexin-43 upregulation, synchronous beating |
| **Topographical Cues** | Microgrooves (width: 5–50 µm) | Anisotropic alignment via contact guidance |
| **Biochemical (Growth Factors)** | TGF-β, IGF-1, BMP-4 | Cardiomyocyte differentiation, maturation |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Laplace's Law (Wall Stress)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot h}$ | Wall stress in a spherical/cylindrical chamber; P = pressure, r = radius, h = wall thickness |
| **Oxygen Diffusion (Fick's 2nd Law)** | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Oxygen concentration profile through scaffold thickness |
| **Steady-State Oxygen Diffusion Limit** | $L_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot C_{0}}{Q}}$ | Maximal viable scaffold thickness; D = O₂ diffusivity (~2 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s), C₀ = surface O₂ conc., Q = O₂ consumption rate |
| **Force per Cross-Sectional Area** | $F_{tissue} = \rho \cdot A_{c} \cdot f_{b}$ | Tissue force generation; ρ = cell density, A_c = cross-section, f_b = force per beat |
| **Scaffold Degradation (First-Order)** | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k \cdot M$ | Mass loss over time; k = degradation rate constant |
| **Young's Modulus from Stress-Strain** | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Stiffness of engineered scaffold |
| **Conduction Velocity (Cable Theory)** | $\theta = \sqrt{\dfrac{d \cdot i_{m}}{r_{i} \cdot c_{m}}}$ | θ = velocity, d = fibre diameter, i_m = membrane current, r_i = intracellular resistance, c_m = capacitance |
| **Cell Seeding Efficiency** | $\eta_{seed} = \dfrac{N_{adhered}}{N_{seeded}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of cells that adhere after seeding |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU exams, when asked for formulas, always **write the equation AND define every variable**. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for undefined symbols.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cardiac tissue engineering serves multiple high-impact engineering applications:

- **Translational Medicine:** Bioengineered heart patches for post-MI patients.
- **Drug Discovery:** **"Heart-on-a-chip"** platforms using iPSC-CMs for cardiotoxicity screening — reduces drug development cost by ~**$1 billion per drug** on average.
- **Disease Modelling:** Patient-specific iPSC-CMs enable study of inherited cardiomyopathies (e.g., hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, long QT syndrome).
- **Bioprinting:** 3D bioprinting of vascularised cardiac constructs using multi-material extrusion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Viable Scaffold Thickness from Oxygen Diffusion Constraints

**Problem Context:** Given the high oxygen consumption rate of cardiomyocytes and the limited diffusion of oxygen through hydrogels/scaffolds, derive an expression for the **maximum scaffold thickness** that can support cell viability without internal vascularisation.

**Step 1: State the Physical Setup**

Consider a planar cardiac tissue construct of thickness $2L$ (slab geometry, with the surface exposed to oxygen on both sides). Oxygen diffuses from both surfaces inward, while cells throughout the volume consume oxygen at a constant volumetric rate $Q$ [mol/(m³·s)].

**Step 2: Apply Fick's Second Law at Steady State**

At steady state, the time derivative vanishes:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = 0$$

So the diffusion-consumption equation becomes:

$$0 = D \cdot \dfrac{d^{2} C}{dx^{2}} - Q$$

**Step 3: Rearrange the Differential Equation**

$$D \cdot \dfrac{d^{2} C}{dx^{2}} = Q$$

$$\dfrac{d^{2} C}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{Q}{D}$$

**Step 4: First Integration with Respect to x**

$$\dfrac{dC}{dx} = \dfrac{Q}{D} \cdot x + A_{1}$$

By symmetry (maximum at centre, minimum flux at centre), the derivative at the centre ($x = 0$) is zero:

$$\left. \dfrac{dC}{dx} \right\vert_{x=0} = 0 \implies A_{1} = 0$$

**Step 5: Second Integration**

$$C(x) = \dfrac{Q}{2D} \cdot x^{2} + A_{2}$$

**Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions**

At the surface ($x = L$), oxygen concentration equals the bulk concentration $C_0$:

$$C(L) = C_{0} = \dfrac{Q}{2D} \cdot L^{2} + A_{2}$$

$$A_{2} = C_{0} - \dfrac{Q \cdot L^{2}}{2D}$$

**Step 7: Final Concentration Profile**

$$C(x) = C_{0} - \dfrac{Q}{2D} \cdot (L^{2} - x^{2})$$

**Step 8: Determine Critical (Minimum) Oxygen Concentration**

The minimum concentration occurs at the centre ($x = 0$):

$$C_{min} = C(0) = C_{0} - \dfrac{Q \cdot L^{2}}{2D}$$

**Step 9: Apply the Viability Condition**

For cell survival, $C_{min}$ must be at least the critical hypoxic concentration $C_{crit}$:

$$C_{0} - \dfrac{Q \cdot L^{2}}{2D} \geq C_{crit}$$

$$L^{2} \leq \dfrac{2D \cdot (C_{0} - C_{crit})}{Q}$$

**Step 10: Maximum Viable Thickness**

$$\boxed{L_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2D \cdot (C_{0} - C_{crit})}{Q}}}$$

**Numerical Example (Typical Cardiac Values):**

- $D = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s (oxygen diffusivity in hydrogel)
- $C_0 = 0.2$ mol/m³ (physiological O₂)
- $C_{crit} = 0.05$ mol/m³ (hypoxia threshold)
- $Q = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/(m³·s) (cardiomyocyte O₂ consumption)

$$L_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-9}) \times (0.2 - 0.05)}{3 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$L_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 10^{-10}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$L_{max} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$L_{max} \approx 4.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 447 \text{ µm}$$

**Conclusion:** Without internal vascularisation, a cardiac tissue construct can only be ~**447 µm** thick before experiencing central hypoxia. This **strongly motivates the need for vascularisation strategies** in CTE.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wall Stress on a Cardiac Patch using Laplace's Law

**Problem Context:** A spherical cardiac patch is implanted over a defect of radius $r$ to restore mechanical integrity. Derive the wall stress experienced by the patch.

**Step 1: Geometric Setup**

Consider a thin-walled spherical shell (the patch) of radius $r$, wall thickness $h$, and internal pressure $P$.

**Step 2: Force Balance on a Hemispherical Element**

The pressure force pushing outward on one hemisphere is:

$$F_{pressure} = P \cdot \pi r^{2}$$

The wall tension force resisting rupture on the cut cross-section is:

$$F_{tension} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot h$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium Condition**

At equilibrium, these forces balance:

$$P \cdot \pi r^{2} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot h$$

**Step 4: Solve for Wall Stress**

$$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2h}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Interpretation**

For a left ventricle: $P \approx 120$ mmHg = **16 kPa**, $r \approx 3$ cm = 0.03 m, $h \approx 1.5$ cm = 0.015 m.

$$\sigma = \dfrac{16{,}000 \times 0.03}{2 \times 0.015} = 16{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 16 \text{ kPa}$$

This value must be **less than the yield strength** of the engineered scaffold to prevent rupture.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Bioreactor Types for Cardiac TE

| Bioreactor Type | Mechanical Stimulus | Electrical Stimulus | Cell Alignment | Maturation Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Static culture** | None | None | Random | Low |
| **Perfusion bioreactor** | Shear stress | None | Moderate | Medium |
| **Cyclic stretch (Flexcell)** | Cyclic uniaxial/biaxial | Optional | High (uniaxial) | High |
| **Rotating wall (RWV)** | Low shear, microgravity | None | Random | Low-Medium |
| **Electrical stimulation chamber** | None | Biphasic pulses | Moderate | High (Cx43↑) |
| **Combined mech-electrical** | Cyclic + electrical | Synchronous | Very High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
